thread.title: ASHEVILLE, N.C. (WLOS) — Gov. Roy Cooper traveled
thread.title_full: ASHEVILLE, N.C. (WLOS) — Gov. Roy Cooper traveled

author: Taylor Thompson
title: ASHEVILLE, N.C. (WLOS) — Gov. Roy Cooper traveled
text:
ASHEVILLE, N.C. (WLOS) — Gov. Roy Cooper traveled to Washington, D.C this week with many local Western North Carolina leaders to ask for more funding for Helene recovery.
Cooper requested more than $25 billion in federal funding to help this region recover.
“Not only did we meet with President Biden and his team but nine federal agencies, our US senators, several members of our House of Representatives. We met with budget appropriations staff,” he said.
He said they wanted the entire country to know just how unique and extensive the damage is in Western North Carolina, and that this money is critical in helping the region rebuild.
FEMA TO DETAIL DIRECT TEMPORARY HOUSING ASSISTANCE PROGRAM FOR HELENE SURVIVORS
Cooper said that he saw bipartisan support and that both parties realize how important this funding is.
“They know that these federal dollars are going to be critical to rebuilding the roads in such a way that they are future stormproof[ed] as much as possible, helping to rebuild water systems with that future storm-proofing in mind,” he said. He explained how this region needs more flexibility when it comes to funding because so many communities are connected by private roads and bridges, and most federal rules don’t allow for tax money to be used in that way.
“There seems to be a real acknowledgment that we need the help significantly,” he said.
Cooper said that Governor-elect Josh Stein was there and he has presented the information to President-elect Donald Trump, which is important as both of these administrations will move into power come January. He said that it will take everybody pulling together to rebuild Western North Carolina.
GOVERNOR ROY COOPER CRITICIZES SENATE BILL 382, CALLING IT A POLITICAL POWER GRAB
Cooper was joined by local leaders in Washington, D.C, including Canton mayor Zeb Smathers. He said that the federal government needs to play a huge role in their recovery.
“From the White House to Congress to the US Senate, we made clear that if you give Western North Carolinians the respect and the resources, we can rebuild our region,” he said. Smathers said that the people in this region need to know that they are going to be receiving help very soon.
“If we cannot look people in the eye that have lost everything in Western North Carolina – their business, their home, their loved ones – then we’re failing them. This is not a time for partisanship but pure leadership and doing right by the people of our mountains,” he said.
Cooper also addressed how one of the best things people can do is to come to Western North Carolina and spend some money, adding that many places are open and in need of business.
GOVERNOR COOPER SEEKS MORE FEDERAL AID FOR HELENE VICTIMS IN WASHINGTON, D.C. VISIT
News 13 also asked the governor about the growing need for resources as the temperatures drop and many people are still without shelter. Cooper said that they need people to apply for FEMA assistance, which they can do by going to disasterassistance.gov or calling 1-800-621-3362.
“We know that mountain people love their homes and their lands and there are many people who don’t want to leave and are afraid to leave,” Cooper said.
But, it is important, especially now as the weather gets colder, for people to take advantage of FEMA assistance.
“The government is not going to come in and take away anybody's property. This is not something that people need to be concerned about. We’re worried about their health and their safety,” he said. Cooper said that while more temporary housing is part of the federal request, a lot of that help is available for people already and he is encouraging people to take it. He’s hopeful this legislation will be voted on by Dec. 20.
